name: Horror / Thriller Feature Film
description: Horror-optimized structure with tension-release cycles, jump scare architecture, dread escalation, and the 16-beat horror pattern.

beats.name: Cold Open / Opening Kill
beats.description: An intense opening that establishes the threat. A victim we don't fully know encounters the horror. This is the 'promise' — this is the kind of fear this movie delivers.
beats.purpose: Hook the audience with immediate dread. Demonstrate the threat's power. Set the horror tone.

requiredElements.description: Intense opening encounter with the threat

beats.name: False Safety / Normal World
beats.description: Introduce the protagonist in their safe, normal world. CONTRAST with the cold open. The audience knows danger is coming; the characters don't.
beats.purpose: Establish dramatic irony. Make the audience care about these people before endangering them.

requiredElements.description: Protagonist and group established in safe environment

beats.name: Warning Signs / Unsettling Details
beats.description: Subtle wrongness begins. Small details feel off. Characters dismiss these signs. The audience notices before the characters do.
beats.purpose: Begin building dread through atmospheric unease, not overt scares.

requiredElements.description: At least 3 subtle warning signs the characters dismiss

beats.name: First Real Encounter
beats.description: The protagonist directly encounters the threat for the first time. Not the full horror — a taste. Something that could be explained away but deeply unsettles.
beats.purpose: The catalyst. The protagonist's world cracks. Can they ignore this? They try.

requiredElements.description: First direct encounter with the horror — ambiguous enough to deny

beats.name: Denial / Investigation
beats.description: Characters try to rationalize or investigate. Audience knows more than the characters. Tension from watching people walk toward danger while trying to explain it away.
beats.purpose: Build Hitchcockian suspense through dramatic irony and investigation.

requiredElements.description: Characters debate what they experienced — disbelief vs. fear
Investigation reveals partial truth about the threat

beats.name: First Escalation / Someone Gets Hurt
beats.description: The threat escalates beyond denial. Someone is injured, taken, or killed. The group can no longer pretend everything is fine.
beats.purpose: Raise stakes permanently. Death/danger is real. No going back.

requiredElements.description: Visceral confrontation — someone is hurt or killed
Group reacts with genuine terror

beats.name: The Plan / False Hope
beats.description: Characters formulate a plan to escape or defeat the threat. Brief sense of control and hope. The audience suspects this plan will fail.
beats.purpose: Give characters (and audience) a brief respite. Set up the plan's failure.

requiredElements.description: Group devises a plan — feels logical and possible

beats.name: Plan Fails / Midpoint Reversal
beats.description: The plan fails catastrophically. The threat is revealed to be far worse than imagined. A major revelation changes everything — the rules of the horror are different than expected.
beats.purpose: The midpoint. Everything the characters believed about the threat was wrong or incomplete.

requiredElements.description: The true nature/scope of the threat is revealed
Plan collapses — possibly another death

beats.name: Paranoia / Group Fracture
beats.description: The group turns on each other. Trust breaks down. Accusations, blame, paranoia. The real enemy might be inside the group.
beats.purpose: Internal conflict escalates alongside external threat. Isolation increases vulnerability.

requiredElements.description: Group members turn on each other
Betrayal or abandonment within the group

beats.name: Picking Off / Isolation
beats.description: Characters are separated and eliminated one by one. The group shrinks. Each death escalates in intensity. The protagonist becomes increasingly alone.
beats.purpose: Systematic elimination builds dread. Each death should feel different and worse.

requiredElements.description: 2-3 deaths/eliminations, each escalating in horror

beats.name: All Is Lost / Total Darkness
beats.description: The protagonist is alone (or with one other). All resources exhausted. All allies gone. The threat seems unstoppable. Maximum despair.
beats.purpose: Rock bottom. The protagonist has nothing left but themselves.

requiredElements.description: Protagonist completely alone, resources exhausted

beats.name: Dark Revelation / Weakness Found
beats.description: In the darkest moment, the protagonist discovers the threat's weakness — or realizes the only way to survive. This knowledge comes at a cost.
beats.purpose: The key insight. Not a guaranteed win — just a possibility. A chance.

requiredElements.description: Protagonist discovers how to fight back — barely

beats.name: Final Stand / Climactic Confrontation
beats.description: The protagonist faces the threat directly. Maximum tension sustained. The outcome is uncertain until the very end. This should be the longest sustained tension sequence.
beats.purpose: The climax. Everything built to this moment. Maximum horror, maximum courage.

requiredElements.description: Direct, sustained confrontation with the threat
Protagonist uses discovered weakness to fight back
Moment where it seems the protagonist will lose

beats.name: Apparent Victory / False Ending
beats.description: The threat seems defeated. Relief floods in. The protagonist is safe... or are they? In horror, there is almost always one more scare.
beats.purpose: Set up the final scare. Let the audience relax so you can hit them one more time.

requiredElements.description: Relief and apparent safety

beats.name: Final Scare / Last Twist
beats.description: One last shock. The threat isn't fully dead, a new threat is revealed, or the protagonist realizes they've become the threat. The horror genre demands one last jolt.
beats.purpose: The signature horror ending. Leave the audience unsettled. Set up potential sequel.

requiredElements.description: One final shock — the horror isn't truly over

beats.name: Ambiguous Ending
beats.description: The final image leaves the audience uncertain. Is the protagonist truly safe? Has the evil been contained? Horror endings should haunt, not comfort.
beats.purpose: Leave a lasting unease. The best horror lingers after the credits.

requiredElements.description: Ambiguous final image that refuses to fully reassure

genreRules.rule: Tension-release-tension cycle: never sustain peak tension for more than 5 minutes without a brief release
Jump scares: maximum 5-7 per film, spaced at least 8-12 minutes apart
Use Hitchcock's rule: suspense > surprise. Show the audience the bomb under the table
Deaths must escalate in intensity: each death is worse than the last
The protagonist must earn survival through their own actions, not luck
Sound design is as important as visuals: silence before a scare, ambient dread throughout
False scares (cat scares) should be used sparingly — maximum 2-3 per film
The threat should be shown progressively: shadow → glimpse → partial reveal → full reveal
Isolation increases vulnerability — separate characters from help
Include at least one 'rules of the horror' scene where characters learn what they're dealing with

mainPlot.name: Survival Against the Threat
mainPlot.description: Protagonist must survive an escalating horror while discovering how to fight back
mainPlot.thematicFunction: External horror forces confrontation with internal fear/weakness

subPlots.name: Trust / Betrayal
subPlots.description: Can the protagonist trust the people around them? Internal paranoia mirrors external threat
subPlots.thematicFunction: The real horror is human — distrust, selfishness, cowardice

phases.name: Oblivious Safety
phases.externalManifest: Normal life, no awareness of danger

phases.name: Denial
phases.characterState: Refusing to accept the reality of the threat

phases.name: Reactive Fear
phases.characterState: Reacting to the threat but not effectively

phases.name: Stripping Away
phases.internalConflict: Must face the fear they've always avoided

phases.name: Confrontation
phases.internalConflict: Accepting fear and acting despite it
phases.externalManifest: Active, decisive, using intelligence over panic

phases.name: Scarred Survivor
phases.internalConflict: The horror will always be part of them
phases.externalManifest: Alive but haunted — a different person
